name: Apollo Guidance Computer
desc: The on-board computer for the Apollo missions. It guided the 1969 Moon landing.

name: PDP-8
desc: The first commercially successful minicomputer, made by DEC.

name: Altair 8800
desc: The computer that ignited the personal computer revolution.

name: Apple II
desc: One of the first mass-market PCs, designed by Steve Wozniak.

name: IBM PC (5150)
desc: Set the PC standard we still use today.

name: Commodore 64
desc: The best-selling personal computer of all time.

name: Apple Macintosh 128K
desc: The original Mac. Brought the graphical user interface to the masses.

name: Commodore Amiga 500
desc: Multimedia capabilities years ahead of the competition.

name: PC with Intel 80486 DX
desc: The Windows 3.1 era and the rise of the multimedia PC.

name: PC with Pentium
desc: Windows 95, CD-ROMs, and mass-market Internet.

name: iMac G3
desc: The translucent Mac that saved Apple and redefined computer design.

name: PC with Pentium 4
desc: The GHz race era — clock speed as marketing.

name: iPhone (1st gen)
desc: The phone that permanently changed personal computing.

name: MacBook Pro (Intel Core i7)
desc: A portable workstation for professionals.

name: iPhone 15 Pro
desc: More powerful than supercomputers from 25 years ago — fits in your pocket.
cpu: Apple A17 Pro (6-core CPU + 6-core GPU + Neural Engine)

name: Xiaomi Redmi 12
desc: Mass-market Android phone — proof that 'budget' today means flagship-class power from a few years ago.

name: MacBook Pro M4 Max
desc: Apple Silicon with 16 CPU cores and a workstation-class integrated GPU.

name: Arduino UNO
desc: The open-source microcontroller board that brought electronics to makers worldwide.

name: Raspberry Pi 4
desc: A full Linux desktop on a credit-card-sized board for $35.

name: ESP32
desc: Dual-core microcontroller with built-in Wi-Fi and Bluetooth — the IoT workhorse.

name: ATtiny85
desc: A tiny 8-pin AVR microcontroller — fits on a fingertip, runs simple smart devices.

name: Colossus Mark 1
desc: The British codebreaking machine at Bletchley Park — the first programmable, electronic, digital computer.

name: ENIAC
desc: The first general-purpose electronic computer. Weighed 27 tons and filled an entire room.

name: IBM System/360 Model 30
desc: The mainframe that standardised enterprise computing in the 1960s.

name: Cray-1
desc: The iconic C-shaped supercomputer. Price tag: $8.8 million.

name: Atari 2600
desc: The console that popularised home gaming with interchangeable cartridges.

name: Sinclair ZX Spectrum
desc: The tiny home computer that taught a generation of Brits to code.

name: Nintendo Entertainment System (NES)
desc: Rescued the video game industry after the 1983 crash.

name: SEGA Master System
desc: SEGA's 8-bit answer to the NES — huge in Brazil and Europe.

name: SEGA Mega Drive / Genesis
desc: 16-bit "Blast Processing" — Sonic the Hedgehog's home and SEGA's biggest hit.

name: Atari Lynx
desc: The first colour handheld console — backlit screen, 16-bit graphics, ahead of its time.

name: Nintendo Game Boy
desc: The best-selling portable console of the 20th century. Ran on 4 AA batteries.

name: Super Nintendo (SNES)
desc: 16-bit classics: Super Mario World, Zelda: A Link to the Past, Donkey Kong Country.

name: Sony PlayStation
desc: Ushered in the era of 3D home gaming.

name: SEGA Saturn
desc: Dual-CPU 32-bit console — powerful but notoriously hard to program for.

name: Nintendo 64
desc: First 64-bit home console. Super Mario 64 reinvented the platformer.

name: SEGA Dreamcast
desc: Ahead of its time — first console with built-in modem for online play. SEGA's last console.

name: Sony PlayStation 2
desc: The best-selling console of all time: over 155 million units sold.

name: Xbox 360
desc: Microsoft's breakthrough console, pioneering Xbox Live online gaming.

name: Sony PlayStation 3
desc: Revolutionary Cell architecture — also used as a scientific research platform.

name: Nintendo Switch
desc: Hybrid home/portable console built on mobile technology.

name: Sony PlayStation 5
desc: Hardware ray tracing, ultra-fast SSD, 3D audio.

name: Xbox Series X
desc: Microsoft's most powerful console ever — 12 TFLOPS of GPU performance.

name: Steam Deck
desc: Valve's portable PC gaming device — runs Windows games via Proton on Linux.
